host-2021-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The 'Gender and Communication' passage spells out a list of conditional results: men chatted as much as women at laid-back lunches, women out-talked men in small task groups, men out-talked women in groups of six or more. The headline of the paragraph is the sentence 'new research finds that context is key' — talkativeness depends on the situation, which is exactly what B says.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks 'What does the new research tell us about gender and communication?' — so you need the option that summarises the new finding the passage actually delivers. With a research-summary question, the right answer paraphrases the study's headline conclusion, not a single example from it.

steps.title: Locate the headline sentence
steps.text: One sentence carries the argument: 'But new research finds that context is key.' Everything that follows is supporting evidence — the lunch group, the small task group, the group of six or more. The verb 'finds' plus 'context is key' is the author signposting the main claim.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: who talks more depends on the setting. Casual lunch — about equal. Small cooperative task group — women more. Big cooperative group (six+) — men more. So talkativeness isn't a fixed gender trait; it shifts with circumstances and group size.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Sociometer' = a small device that records conversation. 'Laid-back' = relaxed, informal. 'Task-driven environment' = a group with a job to do. 'Out-talked' = spoke more than. 'Chatty Charleys' and 'chatty Cathys' are stock English nicknames for talkative men and women — the closing line is a wink that both genders can be equally talkative.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option against the paraphrase. A says the research 'reverses' traditional features — too strong; the study doesn't flip the stereotype, it conditions it. B ('links conversational patterns to specific circumstances and situations') is a clean paraphrase of 'context is key'. C says men are less talkative except in business contexts — partly true but only one slice of the findings. D says no gender differences — directly contradicts the small-group and six-or-more findings.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The whole point of the research is that talkativeness depends on the setting (context, group size, task vs lunch) — and B is the only option that captures that conditional framing instead of fixing the answer to one situation.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'men chatted just as much as women' and assume the research is overturning the stereotype that women talk more than men — so 'reverses the features' feels like the headline.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't reverse the stereotype; it conditions it on the setting. In some settings women talk more, in others men do, and in casual lunches they're equal. Step 3 shows the finding is contextual, not a clean reversal.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Men out-talked their female peers in groups of six or more in cooperative environments' and read it as confirming men are mostly quiet except in formal, business-like settings.
distractors.why_wrong: C inverts the finding — the passage doesn't claim men are 'less talkative' as a default. In laid-back lunches men chatted just as much; in big task groups they out-talked women. The pattern in step 3 is situational, not 'men are quiet by default'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on the closing line 'chatty Charleys can keep up just fine with chatty Cathys' is that men and women are equally talkative across the board — 'no differences'.
distractors.why_wrong: That closing line only refers to the laid-back lunch setting. The passage explicitly states women out-talked men in small task groups and men out-talked women in groups of six or more — step 5 shows D directly contradicts those findings.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-031.technique: On 'what does the research say' items in ELF, scan the paragraph for the author's signpost verb ('finds', 'shows', 'suggests', 'concludes') and read the sentence it owns — that sentence is almost always the answer in paraphrase. Then check the options against it; options that latch onto one example instead of the headline are usually traps.
host-2021-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: Vivid concrete examples (the lunch, the small group, the six-or-more group) are bait — they pull your eye to specific situations and tempt you to pick an option that names one of them. The headline lives in the framing sentence 'context is key', not in any single example.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The 'Exercise and Memory' paragraph contrasts a decade of research on long-term effects with the new finding: 'just a single session of exercise can improve memory retention'. The word 'just' frames the news — even a one-off, limited bout helps recall. That matches C exactly.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks 'What is the new information conveyed in this text?' — so you want the option that captures what is reported as a NEW finding, not what was already established. The word 'new' is doing real work here: ignore background, target the novelty.

steps.title: Locate the contrast
steps.text: The paragraph opens with the old story: 'For more than a decade, researchers have connected exercise to better brain health over time.' Then comes the pivot: 'But a recent study found that just a single session of exercise can improve memory retention in the hours that follow.' The conjunction 'But' plus the word 'just' marks the new finding.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Old story: lots of exercise over a long time helps brain health. New finding: even one workout — done at the right moment — can boost how well you remember something you just learned. The novelty is the SMALLNESS and ONE-OFFNESS of the exercise dose, not regular exercise.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Memory retention' = how well you remember something later. 'Single session' = one bout of exercise (not a programme). 'Neurotransmitters' = chemicals brain cells use to signal each other. 'Promote' = encourage or boost. 'A single session' and 'limited amounts of physical activity' are the same idea in different words.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('regular exercise is necessary') matches the OLD background claim, not the new finding. B ('no hard evidence') flatly contradicts the passage — the study DID find an effect. C ('even limited amounts of physical activity may promote recall') is a clean paraphrase of 'just a single session of exercise can improve memory'. D ('periods of regular exercise have positive effects on memory') again matches the background, not the news.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The novelty in the paragraph is that a one-off, limited bout — not a regular programme — already helps memory; only C captures that. A and D both name the OLD claim; the trap is failing to read the word 'new' in the prompt.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'researchers have connected exercise to better brain health' and assume the headline is that exercise must be regular to help — so A feels like the takeaway.
distractors.why_wrong: A is the OLD background story, not the new finding. The new finding (step 2) is the opposite — even a single, limited bout helps. The prompt asks for the new information, which A doesn't deliver.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'experts speculate' from the last sentence as 'experts aren't sure', B can look like the cautious-truth option.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Experts speculate' refers only to the MECHANISM (which neurotransmitters and proteins are involved), not to whether the connection exists. The connection is reported as a positive finding from the study; step 3 shows the evidence is presented as real.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that 'periods of regular exercise have positive effects on memory function' sounds like a fair summary of the whole paragraph and feels safer than C.
distractors.why_wrong: D recycles the older 'decade of research' framing, not the new finding the prompt is asking about. The novelty is the small dose (one session), which step 5 shows D does not capture.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-032.technique: On 'new information' items, look for an explicit contrast marker — 'But', 'However', 'Now', 'a recent study' — and read the sentence that owns it. The answer paraphrases what comes AFTER that pivot, never the background before it. The trap option always paraphrases the background instead.
host-2021-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: When a paragraph opens with established findings before announcing a new one, two or three options will recycle the established findings to feel familiar. Underline the pivot word ('but', 'now', 'recent') on first read so you don't drift back to the old story.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The opening paragraph poses the standard climate-refugee story as a rhetorical question — 'Rising seas are eating away at small islands … right?' — and then immediately answers 'Not so for some of the world's most threatened islands'. That set-up-then-reject move is the textbook way to question a common assumption, which is exactly D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks 'What is the main purpose of the opening paragraph?' Purpose, not content — so you want to identify the RHETORICAL JOB the paragraph does, not just summarise what it says. Purposes are usually: introduce, contrast, question, support, summarise.

steps.title: Locate the move
steps.text: Two sentences carry the whole opening: 'Rising seas are eating away at small islands and will eventually turn their inhabitants into climate refugees, right? Not so for some of the world's most threatened islands, which have grown despite experiencing dramatic sea level rise.' The word 'right?' followed by 'Not so' is a clear set-up-then-overturn structure.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The author states a popular belief (rising seas will doom small islands, creating climate refugees), then immediately pushes back: actually some of the most threatened islands are growing. Function-wise, the paragraph is inviting the reader to doubt the headline assumption that small islands are disappearing.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Climate refugees' = people forced to leave home because of climate change. 'Eating away at' = gradually destroying. The rhetorical 'right?' tag is informal English used to set up something the writer is about to contradict — like saying 'so the story goes — but actually …'. 'A common assumption' (option D) directly maps to the 'right?'-tagged belief.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('stress the gravity') would require the paragraph to deepen the alarm — but the paragraph DEFLATES it. B ('cast doubt on the reality of global warming') is too broad; the paragraph accepts sea level rise as real, only the doom-for-atolls conclusion is questioned. C ('warn about the inevitable impact') again would require the paragraph to confirm the alarm, not push back. D ('question a common assumption in connection with climate change') matches the 'right?' / 'Not so' structure exactly.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The paragraph's job is to introduce a widely held assumption — sea rise dooms islands — and then immediately puncture it, setting up the rest of the article's contrarian evidence. That is the definition of 'questioning a common assumption'.

distractors.why_tempting: The opening line 'Rising seas are eating away at small islands and will eventually turn their inhabitants into climate refugees' sounds grave on its own — easy to read it as the paragraph's main message.
distractors.why_wrong: That grave sentence is the SET-UP, not the message. The 'right?' tag plus 'Not so' (step 2) tells you the author is presenting the alarm in order to undercut it, not to stress it. The purpose is contrarian, not alarmist.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read 'Not so' and 'islands have grown' as the author rejecting global warming altogether.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph does NOT reject global warming or sea level rise — it explicitly mentions 'dramatic sea level rise' as a real phenomenon. What it questions is the SPECIFIC assumption that rising seas will doom the atolls. Step 3 shows the target is the atoll-doom claim, not climate science as a whole.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that an opening paragraph about rising seas and threatened islands must be there to warn the reader about climate impacts.
distractors.why_wrong: Warning about 'inevitable impact' is exactly the assumption the paragraph is questioning. The 'Not so' pivot in step 2 flips the script: the article is going to argue the impact is NOT inevitable for these atolls.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-033.technique: On 'main purpose' items, find the rhetorical structure of the paragraph — set-up vs pay-off, question vs answer, claim vs counter-claim. Tag-questions like 'right?', and pivot words like 'Not so', 'But', 'However', signal a 'set up to overturn' move, which almost always maps to 'questions/challenges a common view'.
host-2021-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: When the SET-UP sentence is alarming and the COUNTER is short, the alarming sentence sticks in memory and pulls you toward options that match it (A, C here). The corrective: re-read with the 'right?' tag in mind — whatever the author set up to overturn is NOT the paragraph's purpose.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The passage reports that Kench's team studied Funafuti atoll over a century of data and concluded '18 out of 29 islands have actually grown' and that the group as a whole 'grew by more than 18 hectares'. That majority-grew result matches B: most islands are larger now than a hundred years ago.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks 'What are we told about Funafuti atoll?' — a fact-from-the-passage question. You want the option that the passage DIRECTLY supports about Funafuti specifically, not a general claim about all atolls. Track every Funafuti detail through the text.

steps.title: Locate the Funafuti sentences
steps.text: Three sentences carry the Funafuti facts. (1) 'Funafuti atoll … is an islet archipelago … made from coral debris.' (2) 'Over the past 60 years, the sea has risen by around 30 centimetres locally.' (3) 'After poring over more than a century's worth of data … they conclude that 18 out of 29 islands have actually grown. As a whole, the group grew by more than 18 hectares.' This third sentence is the load-bearing claim.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Funafuti's local sea has risen ~30 cm over 60 years (normal, not 'more than expected'). Over the last 100+ years, 18 of its 29 islands have GROWN, and the total area has grown by 18+ hectares. So the headline fact about Funafuti is: most of the islands are bigger than they used to be.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Atoll' = a ring-shaped coral island or chain of islets. 'Hectares' = a unit of area (1 hectare = 10,000 m²). 'Coral debris' = broken-up coral. '18 out of 29' is a numerical majority — more than half. 'A hundred years' lines up with 'more than a century's worth of data'.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('surrounding sea has risen more than expected') — the passage says 30 cm has 'sparked warnings' but doesn't say the rise itself exceeded expectations. B ('most of the islands are larger now than a hundred years ago') is a clean paraphrase of '18 out of 29 islands have actually grown'. C ('an example of the predicted impact of climate change') is the OPPOSITE — Funafuti is presented as a counter-example. D ('some islands have experienced little or no sea level rise') contradicts the explicit 30 cm figure.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The defining fact about Funafuti in this passage is the 18-of-29-grew finding from Kench's century-long dataset — exactly what B paraphrases. Funafuti is being used as the EXCEPTION to the climate-refugee story, not an example of it.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read '30 centimetres locally, sparking warnings that the atoll is set to disappear' as the sea rising 'more than expected'.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage gives a number (30 cm over 60 years) but never frames it as exceeding expectations — it's just a fact that sparked alarm. Step 3 shows the surprise in the article is the islands GROWING, not the sea rising more than predicted.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'Rising Seas' as a title and an atoll mentioned alongside climate refugees is that Funafuti must be the textbook example of climate impact.
distractors.why_wrong: C inverts the article. Funafuti is the counter-example — Kench's team chose it precisely because the predicted impact (disappearance) did NOT happen. Step 5 shows the islands grew, which is the opposite of the 'predicted impact'.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'no evidence of heightened erosion' from later in the paragraph, D can feel like it's saying 'maybe sea level didn't actually rise much here'.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage gives an EXPLICIT figure of 30 cm sea rise locally over 60 years and calls it 'dramatic' in the opening paragraph. 'No heightened erosion' is about the islands' response to the rise, not the rise itself; step 4 makes the distinction clear.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-034.technique: On 'what are we told about X' items, ignore options about Y or Z and only score options against sentences where X is the SUBJECT. Numerical claims in the passage (here '18 out of 29', '30 centimetres', 'a century') are the most reliable anchors — pick the option whose wording maps onto one of those numbers.
host-2021-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: When a passage uses a specific example as a COUNTER to a general claim, half the options will treat the example as if it CONFIRMED the general claim instead. The corrective: track whether each option points to the article's pivot (the surprise finding) or to the pre-pivot framing the article is rejecting.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: Virginie Duvat is quoted saying 'There is presently no evidence that these islands are going to sink' and that she and others are 'trying to fight the widespread misconception that sea level rise will mean the end for atolls'. Her core argument is that the fear of atolls disappearing has no current evidential basis — exactly A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks which statement is 'most in line with Virginie Duvat's basic argument'. So you want her HEADLINE position, not just any sentence she utters. Find every Duvat quote in the passage and look for the through-line.

steps.title: Locate Duvat's quotes
steps.text: Duvat has two quoted moments. First: 'There is presently no evidence that these islands are going to sink … she and other researchers are trying to fight the widespread misconception that sea level rise will mean the end for atolls.' Second, later: she contrasts rural islanders (who can relocate easily because houses lack permanent foundations) with urban residents (who would find it harder).

steps.title: Identify her basic argument
steps.text: The first quote is the core position; the second is a secondary nuance about who can adapt. Her basic argument is: the popular belief that atolls will sink because of sea level rise is a MISCONCEPTION, and there's no current evidence supporting it. The relocation comment is a follow-on, not her thesis.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Widespread misconception' = a wrong belief that many people hold. 'No evidence' = currently no data supporting the claim. 'Unfounded' (in option A) = without a basis in evidence — the perfect English match for 'no evidence … misconception'. 'Basically' in option A just means 'fundamentally', not 'simply'.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('the current fear of atolls disappearing is basically unfounded') paraphrases 'no evidence … widespread misconception' exactly. B ('local weather conditions are more of a threat than global warming') matches Kench's storm comment, not Duvat's. C ('Kench's data are not sufficient') — Duvat AGREES with Kench, she doesn't critique him. D ('some coral islands more vulnerable than others') matches Kench's caveat about volcanic islands, not Duvat.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. Duvat's headline position is that the panic over atolls sinking is a misconception unsupported by evidence — and A is the only option that names exactly that. B, C and D pull from other parts of the article (Kench's storm/volcanic caveats), not from Duvat's direct quotes.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to mix up who said what — 'Storms and other disturbances … seem to be more important than sea level' sounds like a Duvat-style claim if you skim.
distractors.why_wrong: That storm-vs-sea-level claim is attributed to KENCH, not Duvat. Duvat's quotes (step 2) focus on the lack of evidence that atolls will sink and on adaptation by islanders. Tracking who-said-what matters here.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the contrast between Duvat and Kench (they're quoted separately) and assume Duvat must be qualifying or pushing back on Kench's data.
distractors.why_wrong: Duvat REINFORCES Kench's conclusion — she says 'no evidence that these islands are going to sink', backing his finding. She and Kench are aligned. Step 3 shows her quotes are on Kench's side of the argument, not against him.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading mixes Duvat's quote with the next sentence about Kench's findings 'not applying to other types of islands, like the volcanic main islands of Fiji, Tonga and Samoa', and D feels like a fair summary.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'volcanic main islands' caveat is attributed to Kench's findings, not to Duvat. Duvat's argument is about the broad misconception, not about vulnerability differences between island types. Step 5 keeps the attributions straight.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-035.technique: On 'in line with person X's argument' items, find every direct quote and indirect-speech sentence attributed to X, ignore everyone else's quotes, and ask: what is the through-line of these statements? Use the most prominent quote as the spine; subsidiary quotes refine it but don't replace it.
host-2021-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: When two experts in the article agree but make different specific points, three of the four options often paraphrase the OTHER expert's claim to bait you. The corrective: literally underline the speech-tag ('says Duvat', 'according to Kench') on first read so each option can be scored against the right speaker.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: Kench says 'There is still considerable speculation that islands will disappear as sea level rises' and Duvat says she and other researchers are 'trying to fight the widespread misconception that sea level rise will mean the end for atolls'. Both are pushing back against the same currently popular belief — which is exactly C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what Kench and Duvat 'share as a mutual concern' — so you want the one thing BOTH are quoted as objecting to or worrying about. Find a sentence from each, then look for the common target.

steps.title: Locate the matching pair
steps.text: Kench's frustration: 'There is still considerable speculation that islands will disappear as sea level rises … Our data indicate that the future of islands is significantly different.' Duvat's frustration: 'trying to fight the widespread misconception that sea level rise will mean the end for atolls.' Same target, different wording.

steps.title: Paraphrase what they share
steps.text: Both researchers are bothered by the SAME prevailing public view: that rising seas will make atolls disappear. Kench calls it 'speculation' that contradicts his data; Duvat calls it a 'widespread misconception'. Their mutual concern is the currently popular take on sea level change and atoll fate.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Mutual concern' = something both people worry about. 'Speculation' = guesswork without solid evidence. 'Misconception' = a wrong idea many people hold. 'Currently popular views' (option C) maps onto both 'considerable speculation' and 'widespread misconception' — both phrases describe a popular but unsupported belief.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('absence of a global call for action') — the passage never mentions a call for action; this is invented. B ('negative attitudes towards research') — nobody in the passage is described as resisting their research. C ('currently popular views on sea level change and its supposed impact on atolls') is a clean paraphrase of 'speculation' + 'widespread misconception'. D ('climate sceptics' refusal') — the passage isn't about climate sceptics; both researchers ACCEPT sea level rise as real, they only contest the doom prediction.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. Both Kench and Duvat are quoted attacking the same target — the popular belief that atolls will disappear from sea level rise — and C is the only option that names exactly that mutual target.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'The challenge for island nations is to figure how they will coexist with their changing islands' as a call for international action — so 'absence of a global call' feels like a missing piece both researchers might lament.
distractors.why_wrong: Neither researcher complains about a missing call for action. Their quoted concerns (step 2) are about a wrong belief, not about under-mobilisation. A invents a shared lament that isn't in the text.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'trying to fight the widespread misconception' and read it as the researchers being resisted, which suggests negativity toward their research.
distractors.why_wrong: What they're fighting is a misconception held by the PUBLIC, not negative attitudes toward research. Nobody in the article is portrayed as attacking or dismissing their data; step 3 keeps the target straight.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'misconception' and 'speculation', D's 'climate sceptics' refusal' feels close — both are about wrong public beliefs.
distractors.why_wrong: The popular belief Kench and Duvat criticise is that atolls WILL disappear — the OPPOSITE of climate scepticism. Climate sceptics deny global warming; the belief here OVER-PREDICTS its impact on atolls. Step 5 shows D inverts the direction of the misconception.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-036.technique: On 'mutual concern' or 'shared view' items between two named experts, find one direct quote from each and look for a common KEYWORD or TARGET ('misconception', 'speculation', 'consensus', 'evidence'). The option that matches both keywords is the answer; options that only match one expert, or that introduce a target neither quote names, are traps.
host-2021-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: When both experts criticise something, it's easy to over-read the criticism — 'misconception' can drift to 'climate scepticism', 'speculation' to 'resistance to research'. The corrective: state the target of the criticism in your own words first ('the public thinks atolls will sink'), then match options to that target, not to the emotional tone.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: Tom Spencer is quoted saying bigger storms 'could be catastrophic in the short term even if they increase the area of atolls in the long term'. That short-term-bad / long-term-good split is exactly the both-negative-and-positive aspects depending on time perspective that B names.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is 'argued concerning the impact on atolls brought by climate change'. So you want a claim about climate change's effect on atolls — not just a fact about the islands. Look for a sentence that explicitly weighs the impact.

steps.title: Locate the argument
steps.text: Tom Spencer's quote carries the argument: 'climate change could result in bigger, more frequent storms. These could be catastrophic in the short term even if they increase the area of atolls in the long term.' Two time-scales, two opposite verdicts: short-term catastrophic, long-term growth.

steps.title: Paraphrase the argument
steps.text: Climate change brings bigger and more frequent storms. In the SHORT TERM those storms can be catastrophic for atoll communities. In the LONG TERM the same storms break up coral and deposit it, growing the atolls. So the impact looks different depending on which time horizon you pick — both bad and good are real.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Catastrophic' = causing great damage. 'In the short term' = in the immediate future. 'In the long term' = over a much longer time horizon. 'Increase the area of atolls' = make the islands bigger. The phrase 'depending on the time perspective' (option B) maps directly onto 'in the short term even if … in the long term'.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('need for international efforts') — Kench mentions a 'challenge for island nations' but the article never calls for international cooperation. B ('depending on the time perspective, it involves both negative and positive aspects') paraphrases Spencer's short-term/long-term split exactly. C ('lack of knowledge about its specific nature') — the article presents data and conclusions, not gaps in knowledge. D ('despite limited research, no ground for concern') — Spencer EXPLICITLY says short-term storms could be catastrophic; D denies that.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The article's argument about climate-change impact on atolls is Spencer's two-horizon claim: catastrophic in the short term, atoll-growing in the long term. B is the only option that captures BOTH halves of that argument.

distractors.why_tempting: Kench's closing 'The challenge for island nations is to figure how they will coexist with their changing islands' sounds like an implicit call for help — so 'international efforts' feels close.
distractors.why_wrong: A 'challenge' phrased internally to island nations is not the same as a call for international action — the passage never names international cooperation or aid. Step 5 shows the article's argument is about the dual impact, not about who should help.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'How Roman architects solved …' — no wait, wrong passage. In Rising Seas: 'There is still considerable speculation' may sound like there's a knowledge gap about specific cases.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Considerable speculation' refers to the PUBLIC's wrong beliefs, not to a research knowledge gap. The article reports concrete findings (18 of 29 grew) and named experts agree on direction; step 3 shows the argument is structured, not uncertain.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember Kench's data and Duvat's reassurance, D feels like a fair summary: research says atolls aren't sinking, so no concern.
distractors.why_wrong: D ignores Spencer's explicit warning that storms 'could be catastrophic in the short term'. The article argues impacts are MIXED, not absent — step 5 catches this. Also, the article doesn't call research 'limited'; if anything it praises Kench's century-long dataset.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-037.technique: On 'what is argued about X' items, hunt for the explicit cause-effect or time-split sentence ('could result in', 'in the short term … in the long term', 'even if'). Conjunctions like 'even if' and 'although' usually signal a both-sides claim, which maps to options that say 'both positive and negative' or 'depending on perspective'.
host-2021-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: When an article ends on a reassuring expert and then adds one cautionary expert near the end, three options often paraphrase only the reassuring side or only the cautionary side. The corrective: read the LAST direct quote about impact before answering — it often carries the both-sides argument the question is testing.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The Pest Management paragraph says natural pest management methods 'keep pest levels low enough to minimize crop losses without posing a major threat to the environment'. That explicit trade-off — fighting pests AND limiting environmental harm — is exactly the balance D names.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks 'What is claimed here?' — so you want a claim the paragraph actually makes, not just something plausible about pest control. Look for the sentence that summarises what natural pest management IS, not what it does in one specific case.

steps.title: Locate the claim
steps.text: The summary sentence is: 'All these methods work to keep pest levels low enough to minimize crop losses without posing a major threat to the environment.' That sentence is the paragraph's definition of natural pest management.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Natural pest management does TWO things at once: (1) it knocks pest numbers down enough to protect crops, and (2) it does this without seriously damaging the environment. The whole point is the dual goal — pest fighting AND environmental safety — neither is sacrificed for the other.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Biopesticides' = pesticides made from biological sources (plants, bacteria, fungi). 'Biological control agents' = living things (predators, parasites) used to control pests. 'Minimize' = reduce as much as possible. 'Without posing a major threat to the environment' is the trade-off clause. 'Balance between pest fighting and environmental impact' (option D) is a textbook paraphrase of that clause.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('environment-friendly pesticides are even more effective than conventional ones') — the paragraph says biopesticides 'break down more quickly' and are 'less harmful', NOT that they are more effective. B ('mealybugs introduced to fight pests') — the mealybugs are the PESTS, brought ACCIDENTALLY, not as a control method. C ('biological control agents first used in South America') — the passage never says where they were first used. D ('natural pest management involves a balance between pest fighting and environmental impact') is the clean paraphrase of step 2's sentence.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The paragraph defines natural pest management precisely by the balance it strikes — low pest levels AND low environmental threat. D names that balance; the other three options misread either the effectiveness claim, the role of the mealybugs, or the geography.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'less harmful to humans and break down more quickly than typical agrochemicals' and slide to 'better' or 'more effective' overall.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Less harmful' and 'break down more quickly' describe SAFETY and PERSISTENCE, not effectiveness against pests. The paragraph never claims biopesticides outperform conventional ones at killing pests; step 5 catches the slide from 'safer' to 'more effective'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'mealybugs … in Africa' and 'natural pest management' is to mix the two — maybe the bugs were intentionally brought in as biological control.
distractors.why_wrong: The mealybugs were brought to Africa ACCIDENTALLY in planting materials and they ARE the pests that 'ravaged the continent's crops'. The natural pest management came as a SOLUTION afterwards. Step 3 keeps the sequence straight.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'planting materials from South America', it's tempting to read South America as the source of biological control methods too.
distractors.why_wrong: South America is named only as the origin of the PEST (the mealybug). The passage never says where biological control agents were first developed or used; step 5 shows C is invented detail rather than passage content.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-038.technique: On 'what is claimed' items in dense expository paragraphs, find the sentence that DEFINES the concept (here 'All these methods work to …') rather than sentences that give examples. The definition sentence carries the claim; the example sentences carry the evidence. Options that paraphrase the definition are usually right.
host-2021-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: When a paragraph mixes a problem (mealybugs ravaging crops) with a solution (natural pest management), three options often swap roles between problem and solution. The corrective: on first read, write 'P' next to the problem sentence and 'S' next to the solution sentence so options that confuse them stand out.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The second paragraph opens with 'Demand for produce free of pesticide residues is driving the increase in biopesticides'. Consumer demand for clean produce → growing biopesticide use. That cause-effect is exactly C: growing use is ultimately due to consumer pressure.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks 'What is further said about biopesticides?' — the word 'further' tells you the answer is in the SECOND mention of biopesticides, in the second paragraph, not in the general definition above. Focus on what's added there.

steps.title: Locate the new claim
steps.text: Two sentences in the second paragraph deal with biopesticides directly. (1) 'Demand for produce free of pesticide residues is driving the increase in biopesticides.' (2) 'biopesticides, which are inherently less harmful to humans and break down more quickly than typical agrochemicals.' Sentence (1) explains WHY biopesticide use is growing; sentence (2) describes their properties.

steps.title: Identify the cause-effect
steps.text: The driving FORCE behind the rise of biopesticides is consumer DEMAND — people want residue-free produce, so farms switch to biopesticides to deliver it. So the answer to 'what's driving the increase?' is consumer pressure, not regulation, not technology, not effectiveness.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Pesticide residues' = traces of pesticide left on food after harvest. 'Driving the increase' = causing the growth. 'Inherently' = by their nature. 'Agrochemicals' = synthetic farm chemicals. 'Consumer pressure' (option C) is a clean paraphrase of 'demand for produce free of pesticide residues' — consumers wanting cleaner food is consumer pressure.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('side effects yet to be investigated') — passage says biopesticides 'break down more quickly' and are 'less harmful', not that side effects are unknown. B ('soon outcompeted traditional agrochemicals') — the passage says use is increasing, not that biopesticides will overtake the industry. C ('growing use ultimately due to consumer pressure') paraphrases 'demand for produce free of pesticide residues is driving the increase' exactly. D ('less innovative than commonly thought') — passage praises biopesticides; never calls them un-innovative.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The further claim in the second paragraph is causal: customer demand for residue-free food is what's driving biopesticide growth. Only C captures the consumer-driven cause-effect; A, B and D each add or invert claims the paragraph doesn't make.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'inherently less harmful to humans' and infer a hedge — maybe there are side effects we don't know about yet.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage uses 'inherently less harmful' as a positive property, not as a hedge — and never mentions unknown side effects. Step 3 shows the second paragraph's focus is on DRIVER (consumer demand), not on safety uncertainty.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'increase in biopesticides' and 'beneficial fungi even go beyond pest killing' and project that into 'biopesticides will overtake conventional agrochemicals'.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes a GROWING share, not market dominance. There's no claim that biopesticides will replace agrochemicals soon; step 5 keeps the size of the claim accurate to 'driving the increase', not 'outcompeting'.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'less harmful', 'break down more quickly' as relatively modest properties, D's 'less innovative than commonly thought' can feel like a fair downbeat reading.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph is uniformly positive about biopesticides — naming new properties like soil-nutrient liberation. Nothing in the text frames them as unoriginal. Step 3 shows the further claim is about cause-of-growth, not about innovation level.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-039.technique: On 'what is further said' or 'what is added' items, identify the NEW SENTENCE between the two mentions of the topic. The first mention defines the thing; the second mention adds either a cause, a consequence, or a property. That added piece is the answer; options that paraphrase the original definition or invent a missing piece are traps.
host-2021-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: When a paragraph names a CAUSE (consumer demand) and then lists PROPERTIES (less harmful, breaks down quickly), three options often pull from the properties list rather than the cause. The corrective: ask 'what is driving this trend?' on the first read — the answer is almost always a single sentence with 'driving', 'due to', or 'because of'.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The Lead paragraph reports that Egyptian eye make-up contained 'two lead compounds that do not occur naturally; they must have been made on purpose' and these compounds 'elicited an immune response'. The passage then suggests the Egyptians may have been aware that the make-up reduced eye infections. That is implied knowledge about mixing lead with other substances to make new compounds — exactly D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks 'What is implied here?' — so the answer is not directly stated but follows from the facts the passage gives. Find the sentence that nudges toward a conclusion the author doesn't quite spell out.

steps.title: Locate the implication
steps.text: Two sentences carry the implication. (1) 'A recent analysis of Ancient Egyptian eye make-up identified two lead compounds that do not occur naturally; they must have been made on purpose.' (2) 'In laboratory tests, these compounds elicited an immune response; it may well be that the Ancient Egyptians were aware of the fact that their make-up would reduce the chance of eye infections.' The phrase 'must have been made on purpose' plus 'may well be that they were aware' implies deliberate chemistry knowledge.

steps.title: Paraphrase the implication
steps.text: The Egyptians used lead compounds that don't exist in nature — meaning they MADE them on purpose, by combining lead with other ingredients. And the article hints they may have known the resulting compounds had medicinal effects on eye infections. So the implication is: they understood that mixing lead with other substances produced useful new effects.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Smelt' = extract metal from ore by heating. 'Compounds' = substances made by combining two or more chemical elements. 'Do not occur naturally' = cannot be found in nature; they must be synthesised. 'Elicited an immune response' = triggered the body's defence reaction. 'Mixing lead with other substances' (option D) is the everyday paraphrase of 'lead compounds that do not occur naturally' — a compound by definition is lead mixed with something else.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('Egyptians knew considerably more about lead than the Romans') — the passage doesn't compare their TOTAL knowledge; Romans were aware of lead poisoning, which the Egyptians' awareness of infection prevention doesn't outrank. B ('Romans acquired most of their knowledge from the Egyptians') — there is no transmission claim in the passage. C ('Roman medical practitioners were unfamiliar with lead's unhealthy properties') is contradicted by 'the Romans were aware of the phenomenon of lead poisoning'. D ('Egyptians apparently knew about the effects of mixing lead with other substances') matches the implication in step 2 exactly.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The implication chain is: the lead compounds don't occur naturally → Egyptians made them by mixing → and the article suggests they understood the medical effects. Only D captures the made-by-mixing-with-other-substances inference; A, B and C either contradict the text or invent comparisons it doesn't make.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'Egyptians made lead compounds on purpose for medicine' and 'Romans were aware of lead poisoning' as a head-to-head, and conclude the Egyptians had more knowledge.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes ONE specific Egyptian know-how (medicinal compounds in eye make-up) and ONE Roman know-how (lead poisoning awareness). There's no scoreboard — both knew different things. Step 5 shows the comparison in A is invented.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'Ancient Egyptians also smelted lead' before 'Several ancient Roman medicines also made use of lead', the temporal order can suggest a transmission line from Egyptian practice to Roman practice.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage never names any transfer of knowledge from Egyptians to Romans. The 'also' just adds a parallel — both cultures used lead — not a causal chain. Step 3 keeps the implication focused on what the Egyptians knew, not on whom they taught.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the alarming-feeling 'Roman medicines also made use of lead compounds' and project that the Romans must have been ignorant of the dangers.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly says 'the Romans were aware of the phenomenon of lead poisoning' — they used lead in medicine DESPITE knowing it could be harmful. Step 5 catches the direct contradiction; C is the opposite of what the text states.

host-2021-verb1-ELF-040.technique: On 'what is implied' items, find the sentence with a hedge word ('it may well be that …', 'they must have …', 'apparently', 'seemingly'). The author is signalling an inference the reader should make. The right option paraphrases that inference; options that report stated facts as if they were the inference, or that invent comparisons the text doesn't make, are traps.
host-2021-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: On implication questions, three options often paraphrase EXPLICIT statements from the passage and only one captures the actual IMPLICATION. The corrective: ask 'what does the text not say outright but expect me to conclude?' before scanning options — that question keeps you focused on the hedge, not on the explicit statements that bracket it.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-031.solution_path: Gap 31 sits in 'PR was often known as "propaganda" _____ to the Second World War, but its use by the Nazis saw this term quickly disappear …'. The blank marks a time relationship — BEFORE the Second World War. The preposition that means 'before' here is 'prior'. Answer C.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is a cloze (gap-fill) question. All four options are short words that could plausibly sit before 'to the Second World War', but only one fits the time-and-meaning logic the sentence needs. Decide the relationship you need BEFORE looking at the options.

steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap
steps.text: 'PR was often known as "propaganda" _____ to the Second World War, but its use by the Nazis saw this term quickly disappear from the industry and turn into a form of abuse.' The clause 'but its use by the Nazis saw this term quickly disappear' tells you that after the war the word 'propaganda' became unusable. So before the war it WAS used. The gap needs to express that pre-war timing.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship the gap needs is BEFORE A TIME POINT — propaganda was the term used in the period leading up to WWII. The English preposition family that pairs with 'to' and means 'before' is 'prior to'. So you need a word that, combined with 'to', means 'before'.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Linked to' = connected with. 'Due to' = caused by, because of. 'Prior to' = before (in time). 'Opposed to' = against, in opposition to. Of these four, only 'prior to' is a time preposition meaning 'before'. The others either mark cause, connection, or opposition — none of them carries a 'before-in-time' meaning.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: Walk each option in the sentence. A 'linked to the Second World War' — would mean propaganda was connected to WWII, but the contrast 'but its use by the Nazis …' requires a TIME contrast (before → after), not a connection. B 'due to the Second World War' — would mean propaganda was the term BECAUSE of WWII; the next clause then makes no sense. C 'prior to the Second World War' — clean time meaning: before WWII it was called propaganda, after it stopped being called that. D 'opposed to the Second World War' — would mean propaganda was somehow against WWII; nonsense in context.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. Only 'prior to' captures the before-the-war timing the contrast clause demands. The whole sentence works only when the gap means 'before': PR used to be called propaganda before WWII, but after the Nazis used it the term became abuse.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'PR was often known as "propaganda"' and 'the Second World War' as two big topics in the sentence and guess a connection between them, making 'linked to' feel natural.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Linked to' marks a connection without time direction; it doesn't deliver the before-the-war meaning the contrast 'but its use by the Nazis …' requires. Step 3 shows the relationship needed is temporal, not associative.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember that WWII transformed many institutions, 'due to' can feel idiomatic — propaganda was a term due to wartime conditions.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Due to' means 'because of' / caused by, but the sentence is contrasting BEFORE and AFTER the war, not naming the war as the cause of the label. Step 5 shows that on plug-and-test the cause reading breaks the second clause.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'propaganda' + 'Nazis' is to read the sentence as criticism of wartime ideology, which makes 'opposed to' feel like it fits the moral tone.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Opposed to' means 'against', but propaganda as a term wasn't AGAINST WWII — it was the LABEL used before WWII. Step 5 catches the meaning mismatch; the moral tone is irrelevant to the preposition's job.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-031.technique: On cloze items, decide the RELATIONSHIP first (time, cause, connection, opposition, opposition) before scanning the options. Read the sentence twice with the gap as a placeholder and ask 'what is the gap doing in this sentence?'. Common time fillers are 'prior to', 'after', 'during', 'before', 'since'.
host-2021-verb2-ELF-031.pitfall: All four cloze options usually sound grammatical with 'to' (linked to, due to, prior to, opposed to) — that's the trap. Pick the one whose MEANING the surrounding sentence demands, not the one that sounds natural in isolation.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-032.solution_path: Gap 32 sits in 'corporations use agencies to deflect _____ away from damaging commercial activities'. The verb 'deflect away from' needs an object — something you redirect AWAY from a topic. The standard collocation is 'deflect attention away from'. Answer B.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is a cloze question testing collocation — which word naturally pairs with 'deflect' and the phrase 'away from'. The four options are nouns, so you need the noun that fits idiomatically and makes sense in the sentence's larger argument.

steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap
steps.text: 'Negative views of the PR industry as a whole are fuelled by the tendency of some big corporations to use agencies to deflect _____ away from damaging commercial activities.' The corporation wants to redirect SOMETHING away from negative behaviour so the public doesn't focus on it.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: Corporations don't want the public to NOTICE their damaging activities. So they hire PR to redirect what people are LOOKING AT or FOCUSED ON — i.e. attention. The relationship is: the agency deflects [the public's focus] away from [bad behaviour]. The slot needs the word for 'focus' or 'public notice'.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Deflect attention' = redirect what people are noticing. 'Deflect employees' = nonsense; you can't redirect a workforce 'away from' a topic. 'Deflect customers' = possible idiomatically, but it would mean steering buyers, not managing public perception. 'Deflect support' = also unusual; support is what you GAIN, not redirect. Of these, 'deflect attention' is the canonical English collocation.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: Walk each option. A 'deflect employees away from damaging commercial activities' — would mean preventing workers from doing harmful work; possible meaning, but PR agencies don't manage workforces. B 'deflect attention away from damaging commercial activities' — the textbook collocation; means redirect public focus away from the bad stuff. C 'deflect customers away from damaging commercial activities' — would mean steering customers AWAY from the company's own products, which is the opposite of what PR does. D 'deflect support away from …' — also backwards; you don't want to deflect support, you want to retain it.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. 'Deflect attention away from' is the standard English collocation and fits the argument exactly — PR's job here is to keep the public's focus off the damaging commercial activities. Only B delivers both the idiom and the meaning.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'commercial activities' and assume the gap is about the workforce — corporations 'deflecting employees' away from problematic work.
distractors.why_wrong: PR agencies manage PUBLIC PERCEPTION, not internal staffing. 'Deflect employees' isn't a standard collocation and step 3 shows the sentence is about hiding bad behaviour from outside view, not redirecting workers.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'damaging commercial activities' and think a corporation might want to steer customers AWAY from buying harmful products.
distractors.why_wrong: Companies hire PR to keep customers BUYING and to keep their image clean — the OPPOSITE of deflecting customers away. Step 5 shows C inverts the goal a corporation would have for hiring a PR agency.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'PR' and 'public support', 'deflect support' can feel like a fit — maybe corporations redirect public backing somehow.
distractors.why_wrong: Support is something you RETAIN, not deflect; deflecting support away from your own activities would be self-defeating. Step 4 shows 'deflect attention' is the fixed English collocation; 'deflect support' is not idiomatic.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-032.technique: On cloze items testing collocation, ask 'what is the standard English noun that goes with this verb + preposition?'. For 'deflect __ away from', the canonical pair is 'attention'. Reading magazines, op-eds, and news articles helps lock these collocations in long-term memory — they recur across HP cloze sections.
host-2021-verb2-ELF-032.pitfall: When the surrounding sentence is about business or commerce, business-flavoured nouns (employees, customers, support) feel thematically right and can win against the actually-correct abstract noun. The corrective: pick the noun that satisfies the COLLOCATION first, then check it makes sense in context — not the other way around.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-033.solution_path: Gap 33 sits in 'promoting the view that the evidence over a particular issue … is not _____'. The PR strategy is to make people believe the science isn't FINAL or DEFINITIVE. The adjective that means 'final, definitive' for evidence is 'conclusive'. Answer D.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is a cloze testing an adjective that pairs with 'evidence'. Each option is a candidate adjective. You need the one whose meaning matches the PR strategy the surrounding sentence is describing.

steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap
steps.text: 'they do so by promoting the view that the evidence over a particular issue – whether tobacco's role in causing lung cancer or the notion that current global warming is mainly caused by human activity – is not _____.' The PR strategy is to manufacture doubt: to make the public think the science ISN'T SETTLED.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: If the strategy is to manufacture doubt about a scientific consensus, the PR firm is saying the evidence is NOT [strong / final / settled]. The adjective in the gap is the one PR wants to deny — 'final, settled, decisive'. The English adjective for evidence that settles a question is 'conclusive'.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Possible' = capable of happening; weird with 'evidence'. 'Preventive' = aimed at preventing something; describes a medicine or measure, not evidence. 'Responsible' = accountable; describes people, not evidence. 'Conclusive' = decisive, settling a question — the canonical adjective for strong evidence ('conclusive proof', 'conclusive findings'). Of the four, only 'conclusive' is a natural pair with 'evidence'.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: Walk each option. A 'the evidence … is not possible' — means evidence can't exist; weak and grammatically odd. B 'the evidence … is not preventive' — nonsensical; evidence doesn't prevent anything. C 'the evidence … is not responsible' — also nonsensical; evidence doesn't bear responsibility. D 'the evidence … is not conclusive' — clean and natural; the standard PR-spin claim is exactly 'evidence is not conclusive' (i.e. the science isn't settled). It even matches the real-world rhetoric of climate-denial campaigns.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. 'Not conclusive' is the textbook hedge PR firms use to imply science is still up for debate. It collocates naturally with 'evidence' and delivers the manufactured-doubt strategy the surrounding paragraph describes.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'is not _____' as a strong denial and pick 'possible' as a strong-sounding word — 'evidence is not possible' sounds emphatic.
distractors.why_wrong: Saying evidence 'is not possible' would mean it CAN'T exist, which is too strong — the PR strategy is to admit evidence exists but downplay its strength. Step 3 shows the target is doubt, not denial of evidence's existence.

distractors.why_tempting: If you fixate on 'tobacco's role in causing lung cancer', 'preventive' can feel relevant — preventive medicine, prevention of disease.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Preventive' describes measures or treatments, not evidence — 'evidence is not preventive' is nonsense. Step 4 catches the part-of-speech mismatch; the adjective doesn't pair with the noun 'evidence'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'tobacco' and 'global warming' is moral — corporations being 'not responsible'. The word 'responsible' jumps to mind.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Responsible' fits PEOPLE, not evidence — 'evidence is not responsible' makes no sense. The moral tone of the paragraph is bait; the gap needs an evidence-specific adjective, and 'conclusive' is that adjective. Step 5 keeps the collocation right.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-033.technique: On cloze adjective items, identify the NOUN the adjective modifies and ask 'what's the standard English adjective family for this noun?'. For 'evidence', the natural adjectives are 'conclusive', 'strong', 'weak', 'compelling', 'circumstantial'. Reading scientific writing builds this collocation muscle quickly.
host-2021-verb2-ELF-033.pitfall: When the surrounding paragraph is about real-world ethics (tobacco, climate change), morally-charged words ('responsible', 'preventive') feel thematically right. The corrective: separate THEMATIC fit from GRAMMATICAL collocation — the gap is grammar-first, theme second.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-034.solution_path: Gap 34 sits in 'debate makes for better discourse … than _____'. The sentence then explains 'pitching one scientist against another makes for a "better" TV debate' than the alternative — agreement. The opposite of debate in this contrast is 'consensus'. Answer C.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is a cloze testing a noun that completes a contrast: 'debate makes for better discourse than _____'. The blank needs the OPPOSITE of debate — what debate is being compared to and preferred over.

steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap
steps.text: 'Media outlets are often easy targets for these strategies because debate makes for better discourse – whether on TV, radio or in written media – than _____. While 99 out of 100 scientists in fields related to climate science may agree that global warming is in large part man-made, pitching one scientist against another makes for a "better" TV debate.' The follow-up sentence makes the contrast explicit: scientists AGREEING (99 out of 100) vs. a staged debate.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The author is saying media prefer debate (two opposed sides) to its OPPOSITE — broad agreement. The English noun for 'broad agreement among experts' is 'consensus'. So the gap needs a word meaning agreement.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Conflict' = open opposition or fighting. 'Respect' = high regard. 'Consensus' = general agreement. 'Distrust' = lack of trust. Debate is itself a form of conflict, so 'conflict' is not its opposite; respect and distrust are emotional stances, not opposites of debate. Only 'consensus' names the absence of debate — when everyone agrees there's nothing to debate.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: Walk each option. A 'debate … better than conflict' — debate IS a form of conflict, so this is incoherent; you can't contrast a thing with itself. B 'debate … better than respect' — respect isn't an alternative to debate; doesn't form a meaningful contrast. C 'debate … better than consensus' — debate (two opposed views) vs consensus (everyone agrees) is the perfect opposite pair, and the next sentence about 99 of 100 scientists agreeing nails it. D 'debate … better than distrust' — distrust isn't structurally opposite to debate; nonsensical here.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The paragraph contrasts MEDIA-FRIENDLY DEBATE with EXPERT CONSENSUS — exactly the structure the gap demands. The clincher is the follow-up sentence: scientists in 99/100 AGREE, but TV prefers staged disagreement. The word for that scientific 99/100 agreement is consensus.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to map 'debate' onto 'conflict' as the strong-sounding opposite of 'discourse'.
distractors.why_wrong: Debate IS a form of conflict, just structured. The gap needs the OPPOSITE of debate, not a synonym. Step 5 catches the type confusion; 'better than conflict' would mean discourse is better than a fight, which doesn't connect to the next sentence at all.

distractors.why_tempting: If you frame the sentence as 'media prefer juicy fights to politeness', 'respect' can feel like the polite alternative the media skip.
distractors.why_wrong: Respect and debate aren't structural opposites — you can debate respectfully. The next sentence specifically points to AGREEMENT among scientists, not respectful behaviour. Step 3 keeps the contrast on agreement-vs-disagreement, not tone.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a paragraph about PR manufacturing doubt is that the public ends up distrustful — so 'distrust' feels like a familiar word.
distractors.why_wrong: Distrust is an EMOTION, not a discourse mode. 'Debate is better than distrust' doesn't form a meaningful contrast and the next sentence again points to AGREEMENT not emotional state. Step 4 keeps the part-of-speech and structural sense aligned.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-034.technique: On cloze items in a 'X better than Y' structure, find Y by reading what comes AFTER the gap. The text usually unpacks the contrast — here the 99/100 sentence names consensus in everything but the word. The right cloze answer is almost always the noun the next sentence is paraphrasing.
host-2021-verb2-ELF-034.pitfall: When the paragraph has strong vocabulary (debate, discourse, conflict, distrust), thematically-related words feel right even when they don't form the required contrast. The corrective: name the contrast explicitly in your own words ('debate vs agreement') before looking at the four options.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-035.solution_path: Gap 35 opens the final paragraph: '_____, PR can also be used positively.' The previous paragraphs argue PR is used to manufacture doubt — a negative framing. The final paragraph PIVOTS to the positive side. The discourse marker that signals a pivot is 'Yet'. Answer A.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is a cloze testing a discourse connector at the start of a paragraph. The blank introduces the final paragraph, which talks about PR's positive uses. So the connector has to manage the transition from the preceding negative discussion to a positive pivot.

steps.title: Read what comes before and after the gap
steps.text: BEFORE: paragraphs that detail PR's negative side — corporations using PR to block legislation, manufacture doubt about science, stage TV debates to confuse the public. AFTER: '_____, PR can also be used positively. It is a tool for communication … When it promotes communication, it can be a force for good.' The author moves from negative to positive — a contrast pivot.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship between the two paragraphs is CONTRAST: PR has been described as harmful, but it can also be good. The English discourse markers that signal contrast at the start of a sentence are 'Yet', 'However', 'But', 'Nevertheless', 'Nonetheless', 'Still'. Of those, 'Yet' is the option offered.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Yet' = however, but (contrast). 'Therefore' = for that reason (cause-effect; introduces a consequence). 'Thus' = in this way / so (consequence, similar to therefore). 'Likewise' = in the same way (similarity). Only 'Yet' fits a pivot from a negative claim to a positive one; the other three would mean 'as a consequence' or 'similarly', which would extend the previous negative argument, not flip it.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: Walk each option. A 'Yet, PR can also be used positively' — clean contrast pivot: despite the negative uses, PR can also be positive. Works perfectly. B 'Therefore, PR can also be used positively' — would mean PR's positive use follows AS A CONSEQUENCE of its negative use; absurd. C 'Thus, PR can also be used positively' — same problem as 'therefore'; suggests the positive side follows from the negative argument. D 'Likewise, PR can also be used positively' — means 'in the same way', which would equate negative and positive uses; nonsense.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The final paragraph pivots from PR's harmful uses to its beneficial ones — a clear contrast move. Only 'Yet' marks that contrast; 'Therefore', 'Thus' and 'Likewise' all imply continuation, not pivot, which would break the article's argument.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'PR can also be used positively' as a logical conclusion from the article — so 'therefore' feels like the wrap-up.
distractors.why_wrong: The positive uses are not a CONSEQUENCE of the negative uses — they're a counter-balance, a contrast. Step 5 catches this: 'therefore' wraps the negative argument INTO the positive claim, which is logically backwards. The article isn't saying PR is good because it's been used badly.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'PR can also be used positively' as a closing remark and reach for 'Thus' as a smooth wrap-up word.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Thus' is a consequence/conclusion marker (synonymous with therefore in this register). Same problem as B — it would tie the positive claim TO the negative argument as a follow-on, instead of pivoting AGAINST it. Step 3 keeps the relationship as a contrast.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read PR as one thing with many sides, 'likewise' feels like 'in addition to those uses, here's another'.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Likewise' signals SAMENESS — but the final paragraph is contrasting POSITIVE use with NEGATIVE use, not adding a parallel example. Step 4 catches that 'likewise' would mean positive and negative are alike, which contradicts the article's whole pivot.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-035.technique: On cloze items testing a discourse connector, identify whether the surrounding paragraphs CONTINUE, CONTRAST, or CONCLUDE. Contrast markers: 'Yet', 'However', 'But', 'Nevertheless'. Consequence markers: 'Therefore', 'Thus', 'So'. Similarity markers: 'Likewise', 'Similarly'. Match the connector to the relationship the surrounding text demands.
host-2021-verb2-ELF-035.pitfall: Smooth-feeling wrap-up words like 'Thus' and 'Therefore' often feel right at the start of a final paragraph because they signal closure. The corrective: ask whether the final paragraph EXTENDS the previous argument (consequence) or PIVOTS against it (contrast) — pivot here, so 'Yet' is the only option.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The first two paragraphs say Agrippa intended a statue of Augustus inside and to dedicate the temple to the emperor — 'but Augustus wisely refused the honor', citing Caesar's assassination over kingly ambitions. The implication is that Augustus didn't want the building to read as a personal power display. That matches B exactly.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is 'implied in the first two paragraphs' — so the answer isn't directly stated but follows from the facts given. Look for a motive or attitude that the reader can infer from what Augustus did and why.

steps.title: Locate the implication
steps.text: The key passage: 'Dio Cassius wrote that Agrippa had intended to erect a great statue of Augustus inside and dedicate the temple to the new emperor, but Augustus wisely refused the honor: Julius Caesar, his adoptive father, had been assassinated due to fears that he had kingly ambitions.' Augustus refuses the personal dedication; the cited reason is Caesar's death over fears of monarchical ambition.

steps.title: Paraphrase the implication
steps.text: Augustus refused to have his statue inside and the temple dedicated to him personally. The reason given is that Caesar was killed because people feared he wanted to be king. So Augustus avoided giving the Roman public a reason to fear HIS power-grab. The implication: he didn't want the Pantheon to look like a display of personal power.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Erect' = build, raise up. 'Dedicate' = formally devote a building to someone or some purpose. 'Kingly ambitions' = the desire to become a king or monarch. 'Display of power' (option B) maps onto 'great statue of Augustus' + 'dedicate the temple to the new emperor' — a personally branded temple would have read as a power statement.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('Agrippa's building was erected directly by order of Emperor Augustus') — the text says AGRIPPA marked the event by laying the foundation; nothing about Augustus ordering it. B ('Augustus did not want people to view the building as a display of power') — exactly matches the Caesar-was-killed-over-kingly-ambitions reasoning behind the refusal. C ('Agrippa was offended by Augustus' reaction') — no offense is described; Augustus simply 'wisely refused'. D ('Augustus never wanted the building to be erected') — he only refused the personal statue and dedication; the building itself went ahead.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The Caesar-assassination reasoning behind Augustus' refusal implies a careful avoidance of appearing to grab personal power. He let the building proceed but blocked the statue-and-dedication that would have made it a monument to himself. Only B captures that implied motive.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'Agrippa marked the event by laying the foundation' as Agrippa ACTING ON Augustus' orders — the new emperor's son-in-law would naturally follow orders.
distractors.why_wrong: The text attributes the project to AGRIPPA's initiative ('marked the event by laying the foundation'), not to an order from Augustus. Step 2 makes the attribution clear; A reads a chain of command into the text that isn't there.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'refused the honor' and read 'refused' as 'rejected' in a way that might offend the architect.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage uses 'wisely refused', a positive framing, and never mentions Agrippa's reaction. The relationship is between son-in-law and father-in-law, working together on the project. Step 5 catches the invented offense; C reads emotion into a politically careful refusal.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'Augustus wisely refused the honor' as 'Augustus refused the project', D feels like a fair summary.
distractors.why_wrong: Augustus refused ONLY the personal honour — the statue and the dedication to himself. The building itself was completed; he just declined to put his face on it. Step 3 keeps the scope of the refusal accurate to the text.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-036.technique: On 'what is implied' items, find an explicit fact plus a stated motive, and infer the attitude that explains both. Here: Augustus refuses + cites Caesar's death over kingly ambitions = Augustus wants to avoid the same fate by NOT appearing power-hungry. The right option names that inferred attitude.
host-2021-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: When a passage mentions a refusal, three options often over-extend the refusal to cover the whole project (D), or invent an emotional reaction in the refused party (C), or invert agency (A). The corrective: identify exactly WHAT was refused and WHY before scoring options.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The passage says Agrippa's Pantheon was damaged by fire in A.D. 80, repaired by Domitian, then 'the old temple was pulled down to make way for the building that stands today'. After all that destruction and pulling-down, virtually nothing of Agrippa's original survives — that matches A exactly.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks 'What is said about the history of the building?' — so you want a claim the text actually makes about the construction history. Look for the timeline: who built, when, what happened to it.

steps.title: Locate the history
steps.text: Four moves carry the history: (1) Agrippa founded the original Pantheon in 27 B.C. (2) 'Agrippa's Pantheon was badly damaged by fire in A.D. 80 and repaired by Emperor Domitian.' (3) Later, under Hadrian (post-117), 'the old temple was pulled down to make way for the building that stands today.' (4) That new building is the one we visit now.

steps.title: Paraphrase the timeline
steps.text: Agrippa's original was damaged by fire, repaired by Domitian, then pulled down entirely so Hadrian could build a brand-new Pantheon. Because the old building was DEMOLISHED before today's Pantheon went up, almost nothing of Agrippa's structure can still be there.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Pulled down' = demolished, knocked down. 'To make way for' = to clear space for. 'Virtually nothing' (option A) = almost nothing — a careful hedge that matches the historian's caution about what physically remains. The passage's 'pulled down' phrase is the smoking gun: a demolished building doesn't leave much of itself behind.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('Virtually nothing of the Pantheon erected by Agrippa remains today') — follows directly from 'the old temple was pulled down to make way for the building that stands today'. B ('Two separate versions existed for a short period') — the text never mentions two coexisting versions; Agrippa's was pulled down BEFORE the new one was built. C ('Domitian wanted a more impressive version') — Domitian only REPAIRED after the fire; the wholesale rebuild was Hadrian's. D ('current building erected when the original burned to the ground') — the fire was repaired; the rebuild was a SEPARATE later decision by Hadrian.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The original Pantheon was demolished — 'pulled down to make way for' the current building. With the old temple physically removed, virtually nothing of Agrippa's remains. The other options misread either the sequence (D), the attribution (C), or the simultaneity (B).

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'repaired by Domitian' and then 'the old temple was pulled down' as a sequence where the repaired and the new buildings overlapped for a while.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says the old temple was pulled down 'to make way for' the new — meaning the old had to go BEFORE the new could rise. There's no overlap; step 3 makes the sequence clear (demolish, then build).

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'badly damaged by fire … repaired by Emperor Domitian' and read Domitian as the impressive rebuilder.
distractors.why_wrong: Domitian only REPAIRED the fire damage; the major rebuild — 'broke new architectural ground' — happened under HADRIAN. Step 5 catches the misattribution; C swaps the two emperors.

distractors.why_tempting: If you collapse the fire and the rebuild into one event, D ('current building erected when the original burned to the ground') feels like a clean summary.
distractors.why_wrong: The fire (A.D. 80) and Hadrian's rebuild (after 117) are decades apart, with Domitian's repair sitting between them. Also, the passage says 'badly damaged', not 'burned to the ground'. Step 2 keeps the three distinct moves visible.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-037.technique: On 'history of the building' items, build a literal timeline of named events ('A.D. 80 fire', 'Domitian repair', 'post-117 demolition under Hadrian') and check each option against it. Verb tense matters: 'repaired' is not 'rebuilt'; 'pulled down' is not 'damaged'. Options that compress separate events into one are usually wrong.
host-2021-verb2-ELF-037.pitfall: When a building has multiple renovation phases with multiple named emperors, half the options will collapse two phases into one or swap attributions between emperors. The corrective: literally number the phases (1, 2, 3) on first read and tag each emperor to a phase before answering.

host-2021-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: The passage says the exterior 'still resembled a traditional Greco-Roman temple' but 'no one could ever have anticipated the magnificent sight awaiting them inside' — a vast soaring space and a 142-foot dome. That traditional-outside / spectacular-inside split is exactly A.

steps.title: Understand the question
